messages.content: why is Ryan talking too much

Jim will give me the real story without the corporate jargon, and Pam knows everything that happens in this office — she'll tell me if Ryan's actually been a problem or if someone's just being dramatic.

*looks at camera*

Yeah. Ryan's talking too much. That's... that's real. 

Here's the thing about Ryan — he just got back from whatever he's been doing, and he needs everyone to know he has opinions. It's not that the opinions are wrong, exactly. It's that he delivers them like he's presenting at a TED Talk when someone just asked where we're ordering lunch. The volume of words is not matching the value of the content. That's a Ryan classic.

The honest answer to your question is: he's overcompensating. He feels like he has ground to make up, so he fills silence with strategy-speak and buzzwords, and then everyone in the room slowly stops listening, which means the actual good ideas — and occasionally he has one — get buried under the noise.

Or we could just... tell him to wait until someone asks before he talks. That's it. That's the whole fix. You don't need a meeting about the meeting. You just need someone to say "Ryan, hold that thought" and mean it. I can do that. I've had a lot of practice with a similar situation that involves more beet farming, so this should be manageable.

Oh, okay — so this came to me from Michael, who wanted me to look into whether there's an actual pattern here or if it's more of a one-time thing.

Honestly? From where I sit at reception, Ryan does tend to hold court a little. Lots of confident observations about strategy and industry trends, usually when people are trying to get actual work done. I've noticed a few eye-rolls from the sales team when he gets going, and Jim has given me that particular look once or twice — you know the one. Whether that rises to the level of a formal concern or it's just... Ryan being Ryan, I think depends on whether it's affecting anyone's work specifically.

Can I ask — is this something that happened in a meeting, or more of a general desk-area situation? That would help me figure out if this is worth flagging to Michael as a real operational issue, or if it's more of a "have a quiet word with him" kind of moment. I want to make sure we're handling it the right way rather than making it into a bigger thing than it needs to be.

In the meantime, I can keep an ear out and note if there's a pattern. That way if it does need to go somewhere, we actually have something concrete to point to rather than just a feeling.

Okay. OKAY. Wow. I just got some incredible intelligence from two of my most trusted advisors — Jim, who is like the cool older brother I never had but definitely deserved, and Pam, who is the heart of this office and also makes the best coffee even though I'm not supposed to say that because apparently it's "just the same coffee maker everyone uses." They both came back with gold. Pure gold. And that is a direct reflection of my leadership, so... you're welcome.

Here's the bottom line, and I want you to really hear this because it's deep: Ryan is talking too much because he is *scared*. He's overcompensating. He came back, he's got things to prove, and so he's filling every single silence with strategy words and tech buzzwords and honestly I get it, I completely get it, because when I first became Regional Manager I also talked a lot — still do, famously — but the difference is that *my* words are always worth hearing. Ryan's words are sometimes worth hearing, buried under a lot of other words that are not. It's like a Kinder Egg. You gotta get through the chocolate to find the toy. That's Ryan. Jim said it best: the volume is not matching the value. Bam. Said the lady.

So here's what we're going to do, and this comes straight from the top — me — with full credit to my team for the research and legwork: someone needs to say "Ryan, hold that thought" and actually mean it. Jim's volunteered, which is great because he has experience managing strong personalities *\*cough\** Dwight *\*cough\**. Pam is going to keep a quiet eye on whether this is a pattern or a one-time thing, because that's what Pam does — she sees everything, she says the right amount, she is basically the anti-Ryan in the best possible way. No formal investigation, no big dramatic intervention. Just a quiet word, some gentle course-correction, and the kind of compassionate boundary-setting that I have literally been doing my entire career. I am, after all, a people person. That's what she said.
